title: Allosidastrum interruptum
intro: Allosidastrum interruptum, also known as 'interrupted calabar bean,' is a plant species native to tropical Africa, belonging to the family Malvaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Allosidastrum interruptum include flavonoids, saponins, and alkaloids, such as calabar bean, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Allosidastrum interruptum is commonly used in herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as in traditional remedies for skin conditions and fever relief.
taxonomy: Allosidastrum interruptum is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malvaceae. It belongs to the genus Allosidastrum, which is a group of plants that are native to the tropical regions of the world. Allosidastrum interruptum is a member of the tribe Malveae, which includes a variety of plants with showy flowers and simple leaves. The plant's classification is further supported by its morphological characteristics, such as its hairy stems and sepals. Allosidastrum interruptum's classification is a key aspect of its identification and understanding within the botanical community.
